g.data-narration: Welcome to Combinations with Repetition, a fundamental concept in counting that allows selecting items multiple times.
g.text: Stars and Bars Method • Counting • Applications

g.data-narration: Combinations with repetition allows selecting k items from n types where each type can be chosen multiple times.
g.text: Selecting k items from n distinct types where:
Repetitions allowed (each type can be selected multiple times)
Order does NOT matter (we care about WHAT, not WHEN)

g.data-narration: The key difference from regular combinations is that we CAN select the same item more than once.
g.text: Key Distinction: With vs Without Repetition
Each item chosen at most once
Each item can be chosen multiple times

g.data-narration: The formula C(n+k-1, k) counts ways to choose k positions from n+k-1 total positions for our items.
g.text: For n types of items, selecting k items with repetition:
Where: n = number of distinct types, k = number of items to select

g.data-narration: Stars and Bars visualizes selection by representing items as stars and type boundaries as bars.
g.text: Visual technique for combinations with repetition:

g.data-narration: Example: selecting 5 fruits from 3 types uses 5 stars and 2 bars in 7 total positions.
Four conditions must be met: homogeneous selections, unordered, repetition allowed, fixed selection size.
g.text: Items of same type are indistinguishable
Each type can be selected multiple times
Select exactly k items from n types

g.data-narration: Type 1: Selecting items with repetition - choose k from n types, like picking balls from colors.
g.text: Example: Select 4 balls from 3 different colors (repetition allowed)

g.data-narration: Type 2: Distributing identical objects among distinct recipients - like giving candies to children.
g.text: Example: Distribute 7 identical candies among 4 children

g.data-narration: Type 3: Finding non-negative integer solutions to equations like x1+x2+x3+x4=10.
g.text: Find solutions to x₁ + x₂ + x₃ + x₄ = 10 where each xᵢ ≥ 0
This is distributing 10 identical items among 4 variables

g.data-narration: Restaurant example: choosing 4 desserts from 6 types with repetition gives 126 ways.
g.text: A restaurant offers 6 desserts. How many ways to order 4 desserts (repetition allowed)?

g.data-narration: Committee example: selecting with constraints requires case analysis - 84 ways to form a committee.
g.text: Form committee of 6 from 5 senior + 4 junior, at least 2 seniors needed.

g.data-narration: Positive integer solutions require transformation: subtract 1 from each variable to convert to non-negative.
g.text: Find positive integer solutions to x₁ + x₂ + x₃ = 15 where each xᵢ ≥ 1
Transform: Let yᵢ = xᵢ - 1, so yᵢ ≥ 0

g.data-narration: Key formulas to remember: basic formula, symmetry property, and transformation for positive integers.
Avoid these mistakes: confusing with regular combinations, forgetting transformation, using permutations incorrectly.
